overview.body: Work through each Gaddis program in order. Chapter 6 is where your programs start talking to the file system. Type every program by hand and actually run each one — you need to see the files get created and read back. Programs 1 through 7 build the foundation; Programs 8 through 10 introduce the with statement, append mode, and exception handling.
objectives: Open a file in write mode and write strings with newline characters
Open a file in read mode and read its entire contents
Read a file one line at a time using readline()
Use rstrip() to remove newline characters from lines read from a file
Convert numbers to strings before writing, and strings back to numbers after reading
Use the with statement for automatic file closing
Open a file in append mode to add records without overwriting existing data
Use try-except to handle FileNotFoundError gracefully
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 6 covers file modes ('r', 'w', 'a'), write() and readline(), the end-of-file sentinel pattern, rstrip(), type conversion for file I/O, the with statement, and try-except for FileNotFoundError.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 6: Files and Exceptions
programRequirements: Each program is saved as its own individual .py file
Each file uses the exact filename listed in the instructions
Run programs in order — several programs read files created by earlier programs
Each program runs without syntax errors and output matches the textbook
overview.filenameNote: This chapter uses 10 individual files. Submit all 10 files together in your final CodeGrade submission. Note that several programs depend on files created by earlier checkpoints — run them in order.

rows.task: Write strings to a file in 'w' mode
Read entire file contents with read()
Write user input to a file
Convert numbers to strings before writing
Convert strings to numbers after reading
Append mode and the with statement
Read records in a loop until end of file

content.body: A program that opens a file named philosophers.txt in write mode ('w'), writes three philosopher names to it (each followed by \n), and closes the file. After running it, you should find philosophers.txt in your working directory. Programs 2 and 3 read this file back.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-1.
Create a new file named file_write.py.
Type the program exactly as shown including the if __name__ == '__main__': guard.
Run it — no output appears in the terminal, but philosophers.txt is created.
Open philosophers.txt in VS Code to confirm all three names are there.

content.title: Mode 'w' Overwrites the Entire File
content.body: Opening a file in 'w' mode destroys all existing content and starts fresh. If philosophers.txt already existed, it is gone. This is intentional here, but it is a dangerous habit to get into for data you want to keep. You will use append mode ('a') in Checkpoint 8 to add without overwriting.

A program that opens philosophers.txt in read mode ('r'), reads the entire file at once using read(), and prints the result. This is the simplest way to read a file — one call, everything returned as a single string.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-2.
Create a new file named file_read.py.
Type the program as shown.
Make sure file_write.py has been run first so philosophers.txt exists.
Run file_read.py and confirm all three names appear in the output.

content.title: The File Must Exist Before You Read It
content.body: Opening a file in 'r' mode raises FileNotFoundError if the file doesn't exist. Run file_write.py first to create philosophers.txt, then run file_read.py. You will learn how to handle this error gracefully in Checkpoint 10.

A program that reads philosophers.txt three times using readline() — once per name — storing each in a separate variable. Unlike read(), readline() fetches one line at a time and includes the \n character at the end.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-3.
Create a new file named line_read.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and observe the extra blank lines between names in the output.
Note why they appear — Checkpoint 5 fixes this.

content.title: readline() Keeps the Newline Character
content.body: Each readline() call returns the line including its trailing \n. When you then print() that string, print() adds its own newline on top — resulting in a blank line between each name. Checkpoint 5 shows how to strip that extra \n before printing.
A program that collects three friend names from the user using input() and writes them to friends.txt. The names are written with '\n' concatenated so each lands on its own line in the file.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-4.
Create a new file named write_names.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and enter three names.
Open friends.txt to confirm each name is on its own line.

content.title: write() Does Not Add Newlines Automatically
content.body: Unlike print(), write() puts exactly what you give it and nothing more. If you write name1 without + '\n', all three names end up on a single line in the file. Always append '\n' when you want each item on its own line.

The same line-by-line read from Checkpoint 3, but with rstrip('\n') applied to each line before printing. This removes the trailing newline character that readline() includes, eliminating the extra blank lines in the output.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-5.
Create a new file named strip_newline.py.
Type the program as shown, noting the three rstrip('\n') calls.
Run it and confirm the output no longer has extra blank lines between names.

content.title: rstrip('\n') Is Standard Practice for File Reading
content.body: Almost every program that reads lines from a file needs rstrip('\n') or strip() to clean up the newline before using the data. Make it a habit: any time you call readline(), strip the result before you print it or compare it to anything.

A program that collects three integers from the user and writes them to numbers.txt. Because write() only accepts strings, each integer must be converted with str() before writing. Checkpoint 7 reads this file back and converts the strings to integers for math.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-6.
Create a new file named write_numbers.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and enter three numbers.
Open numbers.txt to confirm the numbers are stored one per line.

content.title: write() Only Accepts Strings
content.body: Trying to write an integer directly — outfile.write(num1) — raises a TypeError. Files store text, not numbers. Use str(num1) to convert first. When reading the number back in Checkpoint 7, you then reverse this with int().

A program that reads the three numbers written by write_numbers.py and calculates their total. Each readline() call is wrapped in int() to convert the string back to an integer immediately. The total is then calculated and displayed.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-7.
Create a new file named read_numbers.py.
Type the program as shown.
Make sure write_numbers.py has been run first to create numbers.txt.
Run it and confirm the total matches the sum of the numbers you entered.

content.title: int() and readline() Can Be Combined
content.body: int(infile.readline()) reads a line and converts it to an integer in one step. This works because int() strips the trailing \n automatically when it parses the string. You could also do readline() then rstrip() then int() — same result, just more steps.

A coffee inventory program that uses two things introduced together: the with statement (which closes the file automatically when the block ends) and append mode ('a'), which adds records to the end of coffee.txt without erasing what's already there. A sentinel loop lets the user add multiple records.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-18 (6th Ed) or Program 6-15 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named add_coffee_record.py.
Type the program as shown, paying attention to the with block indentation.
Run it and add two or three coffee records.
Run it a second time and add one more record — confirm the earlier records are still there.

content.title: with Closes the File Automatically
content.body: When the with block ends (when the code indentation returns to the main() level), Python closes the file automatically — even if an error occurs inside the block. This is the preferred way to handle files in Python and eliminates the need for a separate close() call.

A program that reads coffee.txt and displays every record using the read-process-read loop pattern: read the first description before the loop, then loop while the description is not an empty string, process the record, and read the next description at the bottom of the loop. An empty string from readline() signals the end of the file.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-19 (6th Ed) or Program 6-16 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named show_coffee_records.py.
Type the program as shown, paying careful attention to where readline() is called.
Make sure add_coffee_record.py has been run first to create coffee.txt.
Run it and confirm all your coffee records appear.

content.title: The Read-Process-Read Pattern Is Easy to Get Wrong
content.body: The loop reads descr before the while, processes inside the loop, then reads the next descr at the bottom. If you move that last readline() outside the loop or forget it, you get an infinite loop or read the wrong data. The structure is: read → while not empty → process → read next.

A file viewer that asks the user for a filename, then uses try-except to attempt opening and displaying it. If the file doesn't exist, the except FileNotFoundError block catches the error and prints a friendly message instead of crashing. This is the standard pattern for any program that opens files by name.

items: Open your textbook to Program 6-28 (6th Ed) or Program 6-25 (5th Ed).
Create a new file named display_file2.py.
Type the program as shown.
Run it and enter philosophers.txt — confirm the contents appear.
Run it again and enter a filename that does not exist — confirm the error message appears instead of a crash.

content.title: try-except Is How You Handle Predictable Errors
content.body: Code in the try block runs normally. If a FileNotFoundError occurs, Python jumps to the except block instead of crashing. The program continues cleanly after the except block. This pattern works for any predictable error — not just file errors.

beforeYouSubmit: file_read.py runs and displays the contents of philosophers.txt
line_read.py runs and reads philosophers.txt line by line
write_names.py runs and creates friends.txt with three names
strip_newline.py runs and output has no extra blank lines
write_numbers.py runs and creates numbers.txt with three numbers
read_numbers.py runs and displays the correct total
add_coffee_record.py runs and appends records to coffee.txt
show_coffee_records.py runs and displays all coffee records
display_file2.py handles both a found file and a missing file correctly
finalChecklist.submissionReminder: CodeGrade only keeps your most recent submission. Your final upload must include all 10 files or you will lose credit for any that are missing.
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: These are Chapter 6 BookEx exercises. You are typing Gaddis programs exactly as written. All programs use the def main() and if __name__ == '__main__': structure from Chapter 5.

specific: rstrip('\n') and strip() for cleaning file data
str(), int(), float() for type conversion with file I/O
The with statement for automatic file closing
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: lists as data structures, classes, anything from chapters not yet covered

ifYouGetStuck: If you get FileNotFoundError, check that the program that creates the file has been run first — several checkpoints depend on earlier ones
If your output has extra blank lines, you are missing rstrip('\n') — readline() includes the newline character
If you get TypeError on write(), you are trying to write a number directly — use str() to convert first
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which files pass and which need work
Post in the course Discord if you are stuck after two attempts (describe the error, do not post your code)
Come to Zoom or office hours if you are still stuck
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdown of every program in this chapter
